title: Food & Restaurants
shortSummary: Order a meal, ask about spice and ingredients, and pay the bill in Hindi.
culturalNote: Food is at the heart of Indian hospitality, and a host will often insist you eat more (और लीजिए, please have more) several times as a sign of care. Many Indians are vegetarian, so shaakaahaari (शाकाहारी, vegetarian) and maansaahaari (मांसाहारी, non-vegetarian) are everyday words on every menu. Spice level matters; you can ask for food kam teekha (कम तीखा, less spicy) if you are not used to it. The thali (थाली), a round platter with several small dishes, rice, and bread, is a great way to sample a region. Traditionally people eat with the right hand, using roti to scoop curry, and the left hand is kept away from food. In many homes shoes come off at the door, and saying the food is tasty, bahut swaadisht (बहुत स्वादिष्ट), delights any host.

keyPhrases.english: I want a plate of lentils

grammar.explanation: Chahiye (चाहिए) means 'is wanted/needed' and the person who wants it takes the postposition ko (or the special pronoun mujhe = 'to me'). So mujhe chai chahiye literally means 'to me tea is wanted'.

examples.native: हमें दो रोटी चाहिए। (hamein do roti chahiye.)

grammar.commonMistake: Saying main chahiye; the wanter takes mujhe (to me), not main (I).

grammar.explanation: To make a polite request, add -iye to the verb stem: laana (to bring) becomes laiye (please bring), dikhaana (to show) becomes dikhaiye (please show). This is the respectful imperative used with aap.
grammar.commonMistake: Using the bare stem (laa, dikhaa) with strangers; it sounds like an order, not a request.

dialogue.setup: Sara orders dinner from a waiter, Ravi, at a Delhi restaurant.

lines.speaker: Sara
lines.native: नमस्ते! मैं शाकाहारी हूँ। मेन्यू दिखाइए।
lines.transliteration: namaste! main shaakaahaari hoon. menu dikhaiye.
lines.english: Hello! I'm vegetarian. Please show the menu.

lines.speaker: Ravi
lines.native: ज़रूर। दाल और पनीर बहुत अच्छे हैं।
lines.transliteration: zaroor. daal aur paneer bahut achhe hain.
lines.english: Of course. The lentils and paneer are very good.

lines.speaker: Sara
lines.native: ठीक है। एक प्लेट दाल चाहिए, कम तीखा।
lines.transliteration: theek hai. ek plate daal chahiye, kam teekha.
lines.english: Okay. I want a plate of lentils, less spicy.

lines.speaker: Ravi
lines.english: Yes, of course. I'll bring it now.

quiz.question: How do you say 'I am vegetarian' in Hindi?
quiz.explanation: Shaakaahaari means vegetarian; main hoon is 'I am'.

quiz.question: Which phrase asks the waiter to make food less spicy?
quiz.explanation: Kam teekha means 'less spicy' and banaiye is 'please make'.

quiz.question: मुझे चाय _____। (I want tea)
quiz.explanation: Chahiye expresses 'is wanted', with the wanter marked by mujhe.

quiz.question: What does 'बिल लाइए (bill laiye)' mean?
quiz.explanation: Laiye is the polite 'please bring', so this asks for the bill.

quiz.question: Arrange: पानी / लाइए (Please bring water)
quiz.explanation: Object first, then the polite verb laiye at the end.
